title: practical english usage - Part 143
content:
(     Onespeaks-Enghsh-... - the meaning is not 'people in general') or English is spoken in this shop,  They speak English in this shop.

- One/You can usually find people who speak English in Sweden.

talking about 'anyone, at any time'. Compare: One and you are only used in this way in very general statements, when we  are 3 one     and   you:    only    used    in  generalisations English than in British English.

is avoided by many people for this reason. It is less common in American One is often considered typical of more upper-class and intellectual usage, and people the truth.

If one wishes to make oneself thoroughly unpopular, one has merely to tell If you want to make people angry, you just have to tell them the truth.

One is more formal than you (and more common in writing than in speech).

2  one     and   you:    formality       and   class One/You should never give people advice.

One/You cannot learn a language in six weeks.

and hearer.

We   can use one or you to talk about people in general, including the speaker 1  one     and   you:    meaning in  general 181  one,    you     and   they:    used    for   people grammar •  182   one (substitute word): a big one But we  cannot leave out one(s) if there is an adjective.

I think my dog's the fastest (one).

Let's have another (one). Either (one) will suit me.

'Which (one) would you like? 'This (one) looks the nicest.' neither and superlatives.

We   can leave out one(s) immediately after which, this, that, another, either, 5  which       (one),    this     (one),    etc not If you haven't got fresh juice, I'll take canned (juice), (     . .. eanned one.) If you haven't got a fresh chicken I'll take a frozen one.

We   do not use one(s) for uncountable nouns. Compare: 4 uncountable         nouns 'What sort of sweets do you like?' 'Ones with chocolate inside.' Green apples often taste better than red ones.

'I'd like to try on those shoes.' 'Which ones? 'The ones in the window.' One has a plural ones.

We'd like one with a garden, (     ... a one with a garden.) We're looking for a house. We'd like a small one with a garden.

We   drop a if there is no adjective. Compare: 2  a  .. . one 'Can you lend me a pen? 'Sorry, I haven't got one.' I'd like a cake. A big one with lots of cream.

'Which is your boy?* 'The one in the blue coat.' We   often use one instead of repeating a singular countable noun.

182  one     (substitute       word):   a  big    one not {        Qnesays-...) They say her husband's been seeing that Smith woman again.

Note also the common expression they say (= people say).

I bet they put taxes up next year.

They're going to widen the road soon.

They don't like strangers round here.

neighbours, the people around, the authorities).

It usually refers to a particular but rather vague group (for example the They has a rather different, less general kind of meaning than one and you.

also avoided (►  328.2).

However, the use of he/him/his for people in general is now seen as sexist and One should always try to keep his temper.

American English generally avoids this, traditionally preferring he/him/his.

One should always try to keep one's temper.

British English uses one/one's for a reference back.

grammar •  182   one (substitute word): a big one not Let's go and ask the old man for advice, (     .. ,-ask-the-old one...) cannot normally be used in other cases.

We   use one(s) to avoid repeating a noun which  has been mentioned before. It 9 θne(s)     always     refers     back.

Do you need coffee cups or tea cups? (     ... or tea ones?) One(s) is not generally used after noun modifiers.

8  noun    modifiers not (     .. .-than-Diekens^-ones / the-ones-of Dickens?) Trollope's novels are more entertaining than those of Dickens.

A grandparent's Job is easier than that of a parent, (     ... than the one of a not A grandparent's job is easier than a parent's, (     ... than-a parent's one.) either just drop one(s), or use a structure with that/those of (more formal).

One(s) is not normally used after a noun with possessive 's. Instead, we  can 7  that     of not (     . ..four-white) 'Has the cat had her kittens? 'Yes, she's had four white ones.' not I bought some sweet ones today, (     I-bought-some sweet -today.-) not I'll wear my old one. (     .. . my eld.) But one(s) is used if there is an adjective.
